title: Python for Data Engineers: Junior-Level Track
description: Build Python capability from the ground up: data structures, functions, file I/O, error handling, data validation, APIs, and testing for production data pipelines.

chapters.title: Why Python Matters for Data Engineering
chapters.description: Understand why Python is the dominant language for data engineering, how data engineering Python differs from general-purpose Python, and how the CelticWind Energy scenario maps to a real-world data engineering context.

blocks.content: Before learning the tools, it helps to understand the job. A data engineer builds and maintains the systems that move data from where it is generated to where it is useful. Raw data arrives from many sources — smart meter readings, sensor telemetry, weather forecasts, market prices, customer records. That raw data is messy: files arrive with missing values, timestamps in different timezones, duplicate records from overlapping uploads, and columns that change name without warning. The data engineer's job is to build pipelines that take this messy raw data, clean it, validate it, transform it, and deliver it to the people and systems that need it — analysts building dashboards, machine learning models making predictions, and billing systems generating invoices.
The Pipeline Concept

A pipeline is a program that reads data from a source, processes it through a series of steps, and writes the result to a destination. The word 'pipeline' comes from the same idea as a water pipeline: data flows through a series of connected stages, each stage doing one job. The first stage reads the raw data. The next stage validates it — checking for missing values, impossible numbers, and format errors. The next stage transforms it — converting types, normalizing timestamps, computing derived values. The final stage writes the cleaned, transformed data to its destination. Each stage passes its output to the next stage's input, forming a chain.

blocks.title: A simple data pipeline
blocks.content:
Source        Read         Validate       Transform       Write        Destination
(CSV file) → (parse rows) → (check types, → (convert to  → (write to   → (Silver
                              remove nulls)   proper types,   Parquet)      layer)
                                              add derived
                                              columns)
blocks.caption: A pipeline is a chain of stages. Each stage has one job: read, validate, transform, or write. Data flows through the stages from left to right. If any stage fails, the pipeline stops and logs the error — the data engineer investigates in the morning.

blocks.content: The key difference between a data engineer and a data analyst is what they build. An analyst writes a query that answers a question: 'What was the total energy consumption in Dublin last month?' A data engineer builds the system that makes that query possible — the pipeline that collects the meter readings, cleans them, validates them, and loads them into the database where the analyst's query runs. Without the pipeline, the analyst has no data to query. This course teaches you to build that pipeline in Python.

blocks.title: Pipelines run without you
blocks.content: The most important difference between a script you run manually and a pipeline is that pipelines run unattended — typically at 03:00 when nobody is watching. This means every pipeline must handle errors gracefully, log what it does, and make failures diagnosable from the logs alone. These disciplines — error handling, logging, and testability — are the core of what this course teaches beyond basic Python.

Python is the most widely used language in data engineering. If you look at job postings for data engineers, the vast majority list Python as a required skill — more than SQL, more than Java, more than Scala. This is not because Python is the fastest language (it is not) or because it has the best type system (it does not). Python dominates data engineering because of three practical advantages: readability, ecosystem, and glue.
Readability

Pipeline code is read far more often than it is written. When a pipeline fails at 03:00 and the on-call engineer opens the code to diagnose the problem, they need to understand what the code does immediately — not puzzle over cryptic syntax. Python's readability makes this possible. A line like for reading in meter_readings if reading['daily_kw'] > 0 reads almost like English: 'for each reading in the meter readings where the daily kilowatts are greater than zero.' This readability is not a luxury — it is an operational necessity for code that teams maintain over years.
Ecosystem

Python has a library for every data engineering task. Need to read a Parquet file? pyarrow. Need to call a REST API? requests. Need to validate data against a schema? pydantic. Need to orchestrate a pipeline across multiple steps? Airflow. Need to process terabytes of data across a cluster? PySpark. Need to transform SQL models? dbt. Each of these libraries is a Python package — you install it with pip and import it into your code. The ecosystem means you do not have to build everything from scratch. You build with tested, production-proven components.
Glue

Data engineering involves connecting many different systems: reading CSV files from an SFTP server, calling a JSON API for weather data, writing Parquet files to a cloud data lake, and sending alerts to a Slack channel when something goes wrong. Python excels as the glue language that connects these diverse systems. A single Python script can read from a CSV, call an API, transform the data, write to Parquet, and send a Slack notification — all using well-maintained libraries. No other language makes this kind of multi-system integration as straightforward.

blocks.title: Python is not the only option — but it is the default
blocks.content: Some data engineering teams use Scala (for Spark performance), Java (for Kafka ecosystem integration), or Go (for infrastructure tooling). These are valid choices for specific use cases. But Python is the default starting point — the language that most teams choose first, and the language that most frameworks (Airflow, dbt, PySpark) use as their primary interface. Learning Python for data engineering gives you the widest access to tools, jobs, and teams.

sections.title: Data Engineering Python vs Script Python

blocks.content: You already know how to write Python. You can define variables, write functions, loop through lists, and read files with open(). This course does not re-teach those basics. What this course teaches is the gap between Python that works on your laptop and Python that runs reliably in production — the difference between a script that processes a file once and a pipeline that processes files every night for years without breaking.
The Six Gaps

rows: Files are in the current directory
File paths are configured, not hardcoded
Data is a list of lists
Data is typed, validated, and structured as dataclasses
Code is organized into modules with clear responsibilities
Errors are caught, logged, and the pipeline recovers or fails gracefully
Data is assumed to be correct
Data is validated at the boundary — bad records are quarantined, not silently processed
Testing is manual: 'I ran it and it looked right'
Testing is automated: pytest verifies every function with known inputs and expected outputs

blocks.content: Each row in this table is a gap that causes real production incidents. A hardcoded file path works on your laptop but breaks when the pipeline runs on the server (where the path is different). A list-of-lists representation makes it easy to accidentally swap two columns without any error. A single-file script that grows to 2,000 lines becomes unmaintainable. An unhandled error at 03:00 produces a stack trace that nobody sees. Unvalidated data with a negative energy reading produces a billing credit instead of a charge. An untested transformation that silently rounds numbers to the wrong precision costs thousands of euros. This course closes each gap.
The Course Journey

The table above is also the course map. The six gaps correspond to six modules (3-4, 7-8, 10), with the remaining modules providing the supporting skills: the Python toolbox (Module 2), file I/O for the three data formats (Module 5), timezone handling for multi-region data (Module 6), and API integration for external data sources (Module 9). By the end of the course, every gap in the table will be closed — and you will be writing Python that a production team would trust to run at 03:00.

blocks.title: This is not a beginner Python course
blocks.content: This course assumes you can write a for loop, define a function, use a dictionary, and read a file with open(). If these concepts are unfamiliar, start with a general Python course first. This course builds on those foundations to teach the data engineering patterns that production pipelines demand.

Every lesson in this course uses the same running example: CelticWind Energy, a fictional Irish company that operates offshore wind farms and tidal energy arrays along the Atlantic coast. CelticWind serves 400,000 residential and commercial customers across four regions — Dublin, Cork, Galway, and Belfast — and operates 2,400 turbines: 1,800 offshore wind turbines and 600 tidal turbines. The company is part of the European Energy Data Alliance (EEDA), a consortium of energy companies that share a common data standard for meter readings, telemetry, and grid operations.
Why a Scenario Matters

Learning data engineering concepts in isolation — 'here is how to read a CSV, here is how to handle an exception' — makes each concept feel disconnected. Using a single scenario throughout the course connects every concept to a purpose. When you learn CSV reading in Module 5, you are reading CelticWind's meter readings. When you learn timezone handling in Module 6, you are normalizing CelticWind's Irish and European timestamps to UTC. When you learn API integration in Module 9, you are fetching CelticWind's energy prices from the EPEX SPOT exchange. The scenario gives every technique a reason to exist.
CelticWind's Business

CelticWind generates electricity from wind and tidal sources and sells it to residential, commercial, and industrial customers. The company needs data for three purposes: billing (how much electricity did each customer consume?), operations (are the turbines performing within safe parameters?), and regulatory compliance (what was the total generation and consumption per region for the quarterly filing to the Commission for Regulation of Utilities?). The data engineering team — Aoife (the lead) and Cian (a junior engineer, the role you are learning) — builds the pipelines that collect, clean, and deliver the data for all three purposes.
The Four Regions

blocks.title: CelticWind's four operating regions
blocks.content:
BELFAST
                  (Northern Ireland)
                  85,000 meters
                  420 turbines
                       │
        ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
        │                             │
    GALWAY                        DUBLIN
  (Atlantic West)              (East Coast)
  75,000 meters               140,000 meters
  780 turbines                 600 turbines
  (most tidal)                 (mostly wind)
        │                             │
        └──────────────┼──────────────┘
                       │
                     CORK
                  (South Coast)
                  100,000 meters
                  600 turbines
blocks.caption: CelticWind's four regions cover the island of Ireland. Dublin is the largest by meter count (140,000 residential and commercial meters). Galway has the most tidal turbines (the Atlantic coast provides the strongest tidal currents). Belfast serves Northern Ireland with a mix of wind and tidal. Cork serves the south coast.

blocks.content: The four regions are used throughout the course whenever a concept needs geographic grouping: filtering by region in Module 3, aggregating consumption per region in Module 5, and comparing regional patterns in Module 8's validation exercises. The region codes — DUBLIN, CORK, GALWAY, BELFAST — appear in every meter reading record as the region field, matching the EEDA Data Standard that all energy companies in the curriculum share.

blocks.title: The scenario is realistic but simplified
blocks.content: A real energy company has thousands of data fields, dozens of source systems, and regulatory requirements that fill binders. CelticWind is simplified to the fields and sources that illustrate Python data engineering concepts clearly. Every simplification is deliberate — the goal is to learn Python patterns, not to learn the energy industry. When a concept needs more complexity (timezone handling, API pagination), the scenario expands to provide it.

CelticWind's pipeline reads from five data sources. Each source arrives in a different format, on a different schedule, and with different quality characteristics. This variety is not artificial — it reflects the reality that data engineers face: no two sources are alike, and the pipeline must handle each one appropriately. Each source maps to a specific module in this course, so understanding the sources now previews the skills you will learn.
The Five Sources

The meter readings are the pipeline's primary dataset — the 400,000 daily records that flow through Bronze, Silver, and Gold layers. The SCADA telemetry provides turbine health data that the operations team monitors. The weather forecasts help predict generation capacity (more wind = more power from the wind farms). The energy prices determine the revenue per kilowatt-hour. The substations table is a reference dataset that links meters and turbines to their grid connection points. Together, these five sources create a realistic pipeline that exercises every Python skill this course teaches.
The EEDA Data Standard

CelticWind is part of the European Energy Data Alliance, which means its core columns follow a shared standard. The columns meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, substation_id, and turbine_id have the same names, types, and meanings across every energy company in the curriculum. If you take the PySpark course after this one, you will see the same column names with a different company (NordGrid Energy in Germany). This consistency is intentional — it means the Python patterns you learn here transfer directly to the next course without relearning the data model.

blocks.title: Every source has a personality
blocks.content: The meter readings CSV sometimes has missing values and duplicate rows. The SCADA Parquet files are clean (the SCADA system validates before writing) but large (each hourly batch is 50 MB). The weather API returns nested JSON with optional fields that may or may not be present. The EPEX SPOT API requires authentication and returns paginated results. Each source's personality teaches a different Python skill — and that is exactly why the scenario includes all five.

CelticWind's pipeline does not dump all its data into one folder. It organizes data into three layers — Bronze, Silver, and Gold — each with a specific purpose, a specific quality standard, and a specific audience. This layered approach is called the medallion architecture, and it is the most common pattern for organizing data pipelines in modern data engineering. Understanding the three layers is essential before writing any pipeline code, because every transformation you build will move data from one layer to the next.
The Three Layers

blocks.title: The medallion architecture at CelticWind
blocks.content:
BRONZE                   SILVER                    GOLD
    (Raw)                    (Validated)                (Business-Ready)
┌──────────────┐        ┌──────────────┐        ┌──────────────────────┐
│ CSV as       │        │ Proper types │        │ Regional billing     │
│ received     │───────▶│ Nulls handled│───────▶│ summaries            │
│ No changes   │        │ Timestamps   │        │ Capacity forecasts   │
│ Strings only │        │ in UTC       │        │ Regulatory reports   │
│              │        │ Duplicates   │        │                      │
│ 'Just landed'│        │ removed      │        │ 'Ready for analysts' │
└──────────────┘        └──────────────┘        └──────────────────────┘
                         Aoife & Cian's
                         pipeline code
                         lives here
blocks.caption: Bronze stores raw data exactly as it arrived — no transformations, no type conversions, no cleaning. Silver stores validated, cleaned data with proper types, nulls handled, and duplicates removed. Gold stores business-ready aggregations and reports. The data engineering team's code transforms data from Bronze to Silver and from Silver to Gold.

blocks.content: Bronze — Raw and Untouched

The Bronze layer stores raw data exactly as it arrived from the source. The meter readings CSV is saved to the Bronze folder without any modification — no type conversion, no null handling, no deduplication. Every column is stored as a string because that is how CSV delivers it. The purpose of Bronze is preservation: if something goes wrong downstream (a transformation bug, a validation error), the team can always go back to the original raw file and reprocess it. Bronze is the pipeline's backup — the source of truth for what the data looked like when it arrived.
Silver — Cleaned and Validated

The Silver layer stores data that has been validated, cleaned, and converted to proper types. The daily_kw column (which arrived as a string in the CSV) is now a float. The date column is now a proper date object. Missing values are handled (filled with defaults or quarantined to a separate file). Duplicate rows are removed. Timestamps from different timezones are converted to UTC. Silver data is trustworthy — an analyst or downstream pipeline can read Silver data with confidence that the types are correct, the values are within valid ranges, and no duplicates exist. The Python code you write in this course focuses primarily on the Bronze-to-Silver transformation.
Gold — Business-Ready

The Gold layer stores aggregated, business-ready data. The billing team reads the Gold regional consumption summary instead of scanning 400,000 individual meter readings. The operations team reads the Gold turbine health report instead of parsing millions of SCADA telemetry records. The regulatory team reads the Gold compliance dataset instead of joining five Silver tables manually. Gold data is derived from Silver data through aggregation, joining, and business logic. In this course, you will build a simple Gold aggregation in Module 3 (computing regional totals from meter readings). More complex Gold transformations are covered in the framework courses (PySpark, Airflow, dbt).

blocks.title: Never skip layers
blocks.content: It is tempting to read the raw CSV and produce the billing summary directly — skipping Silver entirely. This works until it does not: a bad value in the CSV (daily_kw = 'N/A') slips through to the billing summary because no validation step caught it. The three-layer approach forces validation before aggregation. Every record that reaches Gold has already passed through Silver's quality gates. This discipline prevents the most expensive data engineering mistakes — wrong results that look right.

sections.title: The Pipeline as a Python Program

blocks.content: Now that you understand the layers, this lesson shows what a CelticWind pipeline looks like as Python code — at a high level, without diving into the details. The goal is to give you a mental picture of the destination: the code you will be able to write and understand by the end of this course. Each function in the pipeline maps to a module in the course, and each module teaches you to implement that function properly.
The Silver Pipeline — Top-Level View

# celticwind/pipeline.py — the Bronze-to-Silver pipeline
# You will understand every line of this code by the end of the course

import logging
from pathlib import Path
from celticwind.readers import read_csv_meters          # Module 5
from celticwind.validators import validate_readings      # Module 8
from celticwind.transformers import normalize_timestamps  # Module 6
from celticwind.writers import write_parquet              # Module 5

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)                     # Module 7

def run_silver_pipeline(config: dict) -> None:
    """Transform Bronze meter readings to Silver."""
    bronze_path = Path(config['bronze_path'])            # Module 2
    silver_path = Path(config['silver_path'])

    logger.info('Reading Bronze meter readings from %s', bronze_path)
    raw_readings = read_csv_meters(bronze_path)          # Module 5

    logger.info('Validating %d readings', len(raw_readings))
    valid, rejected = validate_readings(raw_readings)    # Module 8
    logger.info('Valid: %d, Rejected: %d', len(valid), len(rejected))

    logger.info('Normalizing timestamps to UTC')
    normalized = normalize_timestamps(valid)             # Module 6

    logger.info('Writing Silver output to %s', silver_path)
    write_parquet(normalized, silver_path)               # Module 5
    logger.info('Pipeline complete')

The pipeline is a sequence of function calls: read the raw data, validate it, transform the timestamps, and write the result. Each function is imported from a separate module (readers, validators, transformers, writers), and each module maps to a course module. The logging calls (logger.info) record what the pipeline does at each step, so that when it runs at 03:00, the morning's log file shows exactly what happened. The config dictionary provides the file paths, keeping them out of the code. This is the pattern — and every module in the course teaches one piece of it.
What You Do Not See Yet

The code above is the happy path — everything works. A production pipeline also handles the unhappy path: the Bronze file is missing (Module 7 teaches error handling), the CSV has invalid rows (Module 8 teaches validation with quarantine), the weather API is down (Module 9 teaches retry with backoff), and the pipeline's transformation logic has a bug (Module 10 teaches testing with pytest). By the end of the course, you will add error handling, logging, validation, API integration, and tests to this pipeline — turning the happy-path sketch into production-ready code.

blocks.title: The code is readable because it is modular
blocks.content: Notice that the pipeline function is only 12 lines of logic. Each function it calls (read_csv_meters, validate_readings, normalize_timestamps, write_parquet) hides its complexity behind a clear name. You can read the pipeline top to bottom and understand the flow without knowing how any individual function works. This modular design is not just good style — it is the pattern that makes pipeline code testable, debuggable, and maintainable. Module 4 teaches you how to write code this way.

sections.title: The Python Ecosystem for Data Engineering

blocks.content: Python's power for data engineering comes not just from the language itself but from the ecosystem of libraries built on top of it. Each library solves a specific problem, and each maps to a skill that this course teaches. This lesson previews the key libraries — not to teach them (that comes in later modules) but to show you the toolkit that the course will equip you with. By the end of the course, you will have used every library in this list.
The Course Libraries

rows: Reading the daily meter readings file
Normalizing Irish and CET timestamps to UTC
Reading SCADA telemetry, writing Silver output
Fetching energy prices from EPEX SPOT
Validating meter readings against the schema

blocks.content: Notice that five of the nine libraries are in Python's standard library — they come with Python and require no installation. The four external libraries (pyarrow, requests, pydantic, pytest) are installed with pip and managed through a requirements.txt file (Module 2). The standard library handles most of the work; the external libraries fill the gaps for Parquet support, HTTP requests, schema validation, and testing. This balance — standard library for the common tasks, external libraries for the specialized ones — is typical of production Python projects.

blocks.title: The ecosystem is also the career path
blocks.content: Each library in this list opens a door to a framework-specific skill. The csv and json skills lead directly to PySpark's DataFrame readers. The logging skills lead directly to Airflow's logging architecture. The pytest skills lead directly to dbt's testing framework. The pydantic validation skills lead directly to data contract enforcement in any framework. Learning these libraries is not just about CelticWind — it is about building the foundation that every data engineering framework assumes you have.

sections.title: How This Course Connects to Other Tracks

blocks.content: This course teaches Python for data engineering. It does not teach PySpark, Airflow, dbt, or any specific framework — those are separate courses with their own scenario companies and their own depth. But every framework course assumes that you can write production Python: structured code, proper error handling, automated tests, and file I/O. This lesson maps the connections between this course and the framework courses, so you can see where each skill you learn here is used next.
The Foundation Layer

blocks.title: Python as the foundation for framework tracks
blocks.content:
┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
│   PySpark    │  │   Airflow    │  │     dbt      │  │   Fabric     │
│  (NordGrid)  │  │ (SaulėGrid)  │  │ (TerraWatt)  │  │  (NordGrid)  │
│              │  │              │  │              │  │              │
│ Distributed  │  │ Orchestration│  │ SQL-first    │  │ Cloud data   │
│ processing   │  │ & scheduling │  │ transforms   │  │ platform     │
└──────┬───────┘  └──────┬───────┘  └──────┬───────┘  └──────┬───────┘
       │                 │                 │                 │
       └────────────┬────┴────────┬────────┘                 │
                    │             │                           │
              ┌─────┴─────────────┴───────────────────────────┴──┐
              │              Python for Data Engineers             │
              │                  (CelticWind)                      │
              │  File I/O · Validation · Error Handling · Testing  │
              │  Datetime · APIs · Project Structure · Logging     │
              └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: This course provides the Python foundation that every framework track builds on. PySpark uses Python for distributed data processing. Airflow uses Python for pipeline orchestration. dbt uses Python for custom models and tests. Fabric uses Python in notebooks. All four assume the skills this course teaches.

blocks.content: The connection is not abstract — it is specific. The file I/O patterns from Module 5 (reading CSV, writing Parquet) are the same patterns that PySpark's DataFrameReader uses under the hood. The error handling from Module 7 (try/except with logging) is the same pattern that Airflow tasks use to handle failures. The testing from Module 10 (pytest with fixtures and mocking) is the same approach that dbt uses for model testing. The API integration from Module 9 (requests with retry and authentication) is the same pattern that Airflow operators use to fetch external data. Every skill transfers.
Who Should Skip This Course

If you already write production Python — if you use virtual environments, type hints, pytest, structured logging, and pathlib in your daily work — you can skip this course and go directly to the framework tracks. This course is for engineers whose Python experience is limited to scripts, notebooks, or academic assignments, and who need to bridge the gap to production data engineering Python before learning a framework. If you are unsure, look at the pipeline code in lesson 7: if every line is obvious to you, you are ready for the framework tracks.

blocks.title: The SQL track is a companion, not a prerequisite
blocks.content: The SQL for Data Engineers track (which uses HydroAlpes as its scenario) teaches SQL from the ground up. It is completely independent of this Python track — neither requires the other. If your team uses both Python and SQL (most do), taking both tracks gives you the complete foundation. If your team uses only Python (some do), this track alone is sufficient for the framework courses.

This final lesson of Module 1 traces the complete CelticWind pipeline that you will understand by the end of the course. Each step references the module that teaches the skill, creating a roadmap for the nine modules ahead. By the time you finish Module 10, you will be able to look at this description and say: 'I know how to build every piece of this.'
The CelticWind Silver Pipeline — End to End

Every morning at 03:00, a scheduler triggers the CelticWind Silver pipeline. The pipeline starts by reading its configuration from a YAML file (Module 2): the Bronze input path, the Silver output path, the API credentials for EPEX SPOT, and the quality thresholds for validation. It sets up structured logging (Module 7) so that every step is recorded in a log file with timestamps, log levels, and row counts.

The pipeline reads the daily meter readings CSV from the Bronze layer (Module 5) into a list of dictionaries — one dictionary per meter reading, with keys like meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, and customer_type. It also reads the substations reference CSV and the SCADA telemetry Parquet file (Module 5). It fetches today's energy prices from the EPEX SPOT API (Module 9) and the weather forecast from the Met Éireann API (Module 9), handling authentication, pagination, rate limiting, and retry on failure.
Validation and Transformation

The pipeline validates every meter reading (Module 8): checking that required fields are present, that daily_kw is a valid number between 0 and 5,000, that the date is not in the future, and that the region is one of the four valid regions. Invalid records are written to a quarantine file with the rejection reason. The pipeline then transforms the valid records (Modules 3-4): casting string values to proper types, normalizing Irish and European timestamps to UTC (Module 6), and computing derived fields like daily_mwh (daily_kw divided by 1,000).

The pipeline writes the validated, transformed records to the Silver layer as date-partitioned Parquet files (Module 5). It writes a validation report as JSON (Module 5) summarizing the run: total records, valid records, rejected records by category, and the pipeline duration. If any step fails, the error handling (Module 7) catches the exception, logs the full traceback, and exits with a non-zero status code so the scheduler knows the run failed.
Testing

Every function in the pipeline is covered by automated tests (Module 10). The CSV reader is tested with a known 5-row file. The validator is tested with valid records, invalid records, and edge cases. The timestamp normalizer is tested with Irish IST, Central European CET, and UTC timestamps. The EPEX API client is tested with mocked HTTP responses. The Parquet writer is tested with temporary directories. The full pipeline is tested end-to-end with a fixture dataset. The tests run on every code change in CI, and the pipeline is not deployed until all tests pass.

blocks.title: This is where you are going
blocks.content: You are 9 modules away from being able to build this pipeline — a production-quality Python program that reads, validates, transforms, and writes data with proper error handling, structured logging, API integration, and automated tests. Module 2 starts the journey with the Python toolbox: virtual environments, project structure, and the conventions that make pipeline code professional.

chapters.title: The Python Toolbox
chapters.description: Master the tools and conventions that professional Python data engineering requires: virtual environments, dependency management, project layout, type hints, and the configuration patterns that make pipeline code portable and maintainable.

blocks.content: When you install a Python package with pip, it goes into a shared location that every Python project on your machine uses. This sounds convenient until two projects need different versions of the same package: the CelticWind pipeline needs pyarrow 15.0 for its Parquet support, but a different project on the same machine needs pyarrow 12.0 because it uses an API that was removed in version 13. Installing one version breaks the other project. A virtual environment solves this by giving each project its own isolated set of installed packages — completely separate from every other project and from the system Python.
Creating a Virtual Environment

# Terminal commands — not Python code, but the commands you type in your shell
# Step 1: Navigate to your project directory
# cd ~/projects/celticwind-pipeline

# Step 2: Create a virtual environment named .venv
# python3 -m venv .venv

# Step 3: Activate it
# On macOS/Linux:
# source .venv/bin/activate
# On Windows:
# .venv\Scripts\activate

# Step 4: Verify — pip now points to the virtual environment
# which pip
# Output: /home/cian/projects/celticwind-pipeline/.venv/bin/pip

# Step 5: When you are done working, deactivate
# deactivate

The venv module (built into Python 3) creates a directory called .venv inside your project folder. This directory contains a copy of the Python interpreter and a fresh, empty package directory. When you activate the environment (source .venv/bin/activate), your shell's python and pip commands point to this local copy instead of the system-wide installation. Every package you install with pip goes into .venv — not into the system Python. When you deactivate, your shell returns to the system Python.
Why .venv and Not venv

The dot prefix (.venv) makes the directory hidden on macOS and Linux, keeping it out of your file listings and your editor's project view. It also signals to tools like Git that this directory should be ignored — you add .venv to your .gitignore file so that the environment directory is never committed to version control. The packages inside .venv can be recreated from the requirements.txt file (lesson 2), so there is no need to store them in Git. At CelticWind, every project uses .venv as the environment name — a convention that makes switching between projects predictable.

blocks.title: Never install pipeline packages into the system Python
blocks.content: Installing packages without a virtual environment modifies the system Python, which other tools on your machine may depend on. A pip install that upgrades a system package can break your operating system's package manager, your IDE's Python support, or another project that depends on the old version. Virtual environments prevent all of these problems. At CelticWind, the first command in every project setup guide is python3 -m venv .venv — before any pip install.

A virtual environment starts empty — no packages except pip itself. You install the packages your project needs using pip install. But installing packages manually on each machine (your laptop, the staging server, the production server) leads to a dangerous inconsistency: you installed pyarrow 15.0.2 locally, but the production server got pyarrow 15.0.0 because you ran pip install at a different time. The requirements.txt file solves this: it lists every package and its exact version, so that pip install -r requirements.txt produces identical installations on every machine.
Installing and Pinning

# Install a package (latest version)
pip install pyarrow

# Install a specific version
pip install pyarrow==15.0.2

# After installing everything, freeze the exact versions to requirements.txt
pip freeze > requirements.txt

# The resulting requirements.txt looks like:
# pyarrow==15.0.2
# numpy==1.26.4
# requests==2.31.0
# pydantic==2.6.1
# pytest==8.0.2
# PyYAML==6.0.1
# ruff==0.2.2
# black==24.2.0

The pip freeze command lists every installed package with its exact version, including packages that were installed automatically as dependencies (numpy is a dependency of pyarrow — you did not install it directly, but it is needed). Writing this output to requirements.txt creates a reproducible manifest. On another machine, pip install -r requirements.txt installs the exact same versions. This reproducibility is critical for pipelines: a pipeline that works locally must work identically on the production server, and exact version pinning guarantees that.
Why Exact Pinning Matters

Without exact version pinning, pip install pyarrow installs the latest version available at the time of installation. If version 16.0 is released between your local install and the production deploy, the production server gets a different version. Most of the time, this is harmless — the new version works the same way. But occasionally, a new version changes behavior: a function's default parameter changes, a deprecated feature is removed, or a bug fix alters the output format. At CelticWind, Aoife's rule is simple: pin everything, upgrade deliberately, and test after every upgrade.
The CelticWind requirements.txt

# requirements.txt — CelticWind Silver pipeline
# Core I/O
pyarrow==15.0.2          # Parquet read/write for SCADA telemetry and Silver output

# API integration
requests==2.31.0         # HTTP client for EPEX SPOT and Met Éireann APIs

# Data validation
pydantic==2.6.1          # Schema validation for meter readings

# Configuration
PyYAML==6.0.1            # YAML config file parsing

# Testing
pytest==8.0.2            # Test framework
pytest-cov==4.1.0        # Test coverage measurement

# Code quality
ruff==0.2.2              # Linting
black==24.2.0            # Formatting

CelticWind's requirements file includes comments explaining why each package is needed. The comments are not required by pip — they are for the next engineer who reads the file and wonders 'why do we have PyYAML?' The comments also group packages by purpose (I/O, API, validation, testing, code quality), making it clear which packages support which pipeline function. When a package is no longer needed (for example, if the team replaces PyYAML with tomllib), the comment makes it easy to identify and remove.

blocks.title: requirements.txt is the project's shopping list
blocks.content: Think of requirements.txt as a shopping list for the project. Anyone who clones the project can run pip install -r requirements.txt and get exactly the same set of tools that the original developer used. Without this file, setting up the project requires asking someone 'what packages do I need?' — and the answer is always incomplete. The requirements file answers that question completely and permanently.

A Python script starts as a single file: pipeline.py. As the pipeline grows — adding validation, logging, API calls, tests — the single file grows to hundreds of lines. At some point, the file becomes unmanageable: scrolling through 800 lines to find the validation function is slow, two developers editing the same file creates merge conflicts, and the test file grows to match the source file's size. The solution is a project layout: organizing the code into directories and files, each with a clear purpose. This is not just tidiness — it is the structure that makes the pipeline maintainable, testable, and deployable.
The CelticWind Project Structure

celticwind-pipeline/
├── .venv/                    # Virtual environment (not in Git)
├── .gitignore                # Excludes .venv, __pycache__, .env
├── pyproject.toml            # Project metadata
├── requirements.txt          # Pinned dependencies
├── config/
│   └── config.yml            # Pipeline configuration (paths, thresholds)
├── src/
│   └── celticwind/
│       ├── __init__.py       # Makes celticwind a Python package
│       ├── pipeline.py       # Main pipeline orchestration
│       ├── readers.py        # CSV, JSON, Parquet reading functions
│       ├── transformers.py   # Data transformation functions
│       ├── validators.py     # Data validation functions
│       └── writers.py        # CSV, JSON, Parquet writing functions
├── tests/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── test_readers.py       # Tests for readers.py
│   ├── test_transformers.py  # Tests for transformers.py
│   ├── test_validators.py    # Tests for validators.py
│   └── conftest.py           # Shared test fixtures
└── data/
    └── sample/               # Small sample files for development
        ├── meter_readings.csv
        └── substations.csv

The structure separates concerns into directories. The src/celticwind/ directory contains all pipeline code, organized by responsibility: readers handle file I/O, transformers handle data transformation, validators handle quality checks, and writers handle output. The tests/ directory mirrors the source directory — each source module has a corresponding test module. The config/ directory holds configuration files that change between environments (development, staging, production). The data/sample/ directory holds small sample files for development and testing — never production data.
Why src/ and Not Just celticwind/

The src/ directory is a convention called the 'src layout.' It separates the installable package (celticwind/) from the project root, preventing a common problem: without src/, running python pipeline.py from the project root imports the local celticwind/ directory instead of the installed package — which means your tests might pass locally but fail in production (where the package is installed, not run from the project root). The src/ layout forces you to install the package (pip install -e .) before running it, ensuring that local behavior matches installed behavior.

blocks.title: The layout is a communication tool
blocks.content: A new engineer joining CelticWind's team can look at the project structure and immediately understand where to find things: pipeline logic is in src/celticwind/, tests are in tests/, configuration is in config/. They do not need to ask 'where is the validation code?' — it is in validators.py, exactly where the structure predicts. A good project layout answers questions before they are asked.

In the previous lesson, the CelticWind project split its code across multiple files: readers.py, transformers.py, validators.py, and writers.py. But how does one file use a function from another? Python's import system connects files together: the pipeline.py file imports the read_csv_meters function from readers.py, and the test_validators.py file imports the validate_readings function from validators.py. Understanding imports is essential because every multi-file Python project relies on them — and import errors are one of the most common frustrations for engineers new to Python project structure.
Basic Imports

# In src/celticwind/pipeline.py

# Import a specific function from a module
from celticwind.readers import read_csv_meters

# Import a module and use its functions with the module prefix
import celticwind.validators as validators

# Import multiple functions from one module
from celticwind.transformers import (
    normalize_timestamps,
    compute_daily_mwh,
    add_billing_period,
)

# Now use the imported functions
readings = read_csv_meters(bronze_path)
valid, rejected = validators.validate_readings(readings)
normalized = normalize_timestamps(valid)

The from celticwind.readers import read_csv_meters statement says: 'go to the celticwind package (directory), find the readers module (file), and bring the read_csv_meters function into this file.' The alternative — import celticwind.validators as validators — imports the entire module and requires prefixing each function call with validators. Both styles work; CelticWind uses from ... import for specific functions (cleaner when you use only 1-2 functions from a module) and import ... as for modules where you use many functions.
The __init__.py File

# src/celticwind/__init__.py
# This file makes the celticwind/ directory a Python package.
# It can be empty — its presence is what matters.
# Optionally, it can define what 'from celticwind import *' exports:

"""CelticWind Energy data pipeline package."""

__version__ = '0.1.0'

The __init__.py file is the marker that tells Python 'this directory is a package, not just a folder.' Without it, import celticwind.readers would fail with ModuleNotFoundError — Python would not recognize celticwind/ as a package. The file can be completely empty (just the presence is enough) or it can contain package-level code like the version string. At CelticWind, __init__.py contains only the docstring and version — keeping it minimal.
Common Import Errors

The most common import error for new engineers is ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'celticwind'. This usually means one of three things: the virtual environment is not activated (so Python cannot find the installed package), the package is not installed (run pip install -e . to install it in development mode), or the __init__.py file is missing (so Python does not recognize the directory as a package). At CelticWind, the project setup guide includes a verification step: after installation, run python -c 'import celticwind; print(celticwind.__version__)' — if it prints 0.1.0, the setup is correct.

blocks.title: Avoid circular imports
blocks.content: If readers.py imports from validators.py and validators.py imports from readers.py, Python cannot resolve the circular dependency and raises an ImportError. The fix: restructure so that the shared logic lives in a separate module that both can import without circularity. At CelticWind, the rule is simple — modules import downward (pipeline imports readers, readers imports nothing from pipeline) and never in circles.

Python does not require you to declare the type of a variable or a function parameter. You can write def validate(data) and pass a list, a dictionary, a string, or anything else — Python does not complain until the function tries to use the value in a way that its actual type does not support. Type hints add optional annotations that document what types a function expects and returns. They do not change how the code runs — Python ignores them at runtime — but they give you and your tools (your IDE, the mypy type checker) information that catches bugs before the code runs.
Basic Type Hints

# Without type hints — what types does this function expect?
def filter_readings(readings, min_kw, region):
    return [r for r in readings if r['daily_kw'] >= min_kw and r['region'] == region]

# With type hints — the types are documented in the signature
def filter_readings(
    readings: list[dict],
    min_kw: float,
    region: str,
) -> list[dict]:
    """Filter meter readings by minimum kW and region."""
    return [r for r in readings if r['daily_kw'] >= min_kw and r['region'] == region]

# The hints tell you:
# - readings is a list of dictionaries
# - min_kw is a float
# - region is a string
# - the function returns a list of dictionaries

The type hints (list[dict], float, str, -> list[dict]) are annotations that appear in the function signature. They tell the reader — and the IDE — exactly what the function expects and returns. Your IDE uses these hints to show autocomplete suggestions, flag type mismatches, and provide inline documentation. If you accidentally pass an integer where a string is expected, the IDE highlights the mismatch before you run the code. At CelticWind, every function in the pipeline has type hints — a convention that Aoife enforces through the linting configuration (lesson 9).
Common Type Hint Patterns

from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional

# A function that returns two values (a tuple)
def validate_readings(
    readings: list[dict],
) -> tuple[list[dict], list[dict]]:
    """Returns (valid_readings, rejected_readings)."""
    valid = [r for r in readings if r.get('daily_kw', 0) >= 0]
    rejected = [r for r in readings if r.get('daily_kw', 0) < 0]
    return valid, rejected

# A function with an optional parameter
def read_csv_meters(
    path: Path,
    encoding: str = 'utf-8',
    delimiter: Optional[str] = None,
) -> list[dict]:
    """Read meter readings from a CSV file."""
    ...

# A function that returns nothing (writes to disk)
def write_parquet(
    readings: list[dict],
    output_path: Path,
) -> None:
    """Write readings to a Parquet file."""
    ...

The three patterns cover most pipeline functions. The tuple return type (tuple[list[dict], list[dict]]) documents that validate_readings returns two lists — valid and rejected. The Optional[str] annotation documents that delimiter can be a string or None (if None, the function detects the delimiter automatically). The -> None return type documents that write_parquet does not return a value — it writes to disk as a side effect. These hints make the code self-documenting: you can read the function signature and understand the interface without reading the implementation.

blocks.title: Type hints are documentation that tools can verify
blocks.content: A comment saying '# readings should be a list of dicts' is helpful but unverifiable — nobody checks that it is still true after a refactor. A type hint list[dict] is verifiable: the mypy type checker (and your IDE) compares the hint against the actual usage and warns if they mismatch. At CelticWind, type hints have caught 3 bugs in the first month alone — all cases where a refactor changed a function's return type but not all of its callers.

The pipeline code in Module 1 (lesson 7) used config['bronze_path'] to determine where to read the meter data. But where does the config dictionary come from? Hardcoding paths in the Python code (bronze_path = '/data/bronze/meters/') works on your laptop but breaks on the production server (where the path is /mnt/datalake/bronze/meters/). The solution: store configuration in a separate file that the pipeline reads at startup. The configuration file changes between environments; the Python code stays the same. YAML is the most common configuration format for data pipelines — it is readable, supports nested structures, and has a mature Python library.
The CelticWind Config File

# config/config.yml — CelticWind Silver pipeline configuration
paths:
  bronze_dir: /data/bronze/meters
  silver_dir: /data/silver/meters
  quarantine_dir: /data/quarantine/meters
  log_dir: /var/log/celticwind

validation:
  min_daily_kw: 0.0
  max_daily_kw: 5000.0
  valid_regions:
    - DUBLIN
    - CORK
    - GALWAY
    - BELFAST

api:
  epex_url: https://api.epexspot.com/v1/prices
  weather_url: https://api.met.ie/v1/forecasts
  timeout_seconds: 30
  max_retries: 3
Reading YAML in Python

import yaml
from pathlib import Path

def load_config(config_path: Path) -> dict:
    """Load pipeline configuration from a YAML file."""
    with open(config_path, 'r') as f:
        config = yaml.safe_load(f)
    return config

# Usage
config = load_config(Path('config/config.yml'))

# Access nested values
bronze_dir = config['paths']['bronze_dir']    # '/data/bronze/meters'
max_kw = config['validation']['max_daily_kw']  # 5000.0
regions = config['validation']['valid_regions'] # ['DUBLIN', 'CORK', 'GALWAY', 'BELFAST']

The yaml.safe_load() function reads the YAML file and returns a Python dictionary. Nested YAML structures become nested dictionaries: config['paths']['bronze_dir'] accesses the bronze_dir value under the paths section. Lists in YAML (the valid_regions with dashes) become Python lists. Numbers (5000.0) become Python floats. The safe_load function (as opposed to yaml.load) prevents code execution from malicious YAML files — always use safe_load for configuration files.

At CelticWind, three configuration files exist: config/config.dev.yml for the developer's laptop (paths point to ./data/sample/), config/config.staging.yml for the staging server, and config/config.prod.yml for the production server. The pipeline accepts a --config argument that specifies which file to use. The Python code is identical in all environments — only the configuration file changes. This separation makes it possible to test the pipeline locally with sample data before deploying to production.

blocks.title: Never put secrets in YAML files
blocks.content: The config.yml file is committed to Git — every team member can see it, and it appears in the repository's history forever. API keys, database passwords, and authentication tokens must never appear in configuration files. The next lesson covers how to handle secrets safely using environment variables.

The YAML configuration file stores non-sensitive settings: file paths, thresholds, region lists. But the CelticWind pipeline also needs an API key for the EPEX SPOT energy price service and a webhook URL for Slack alerting. These are secrets — values that must not appear in code, configuration files, or Git history. If an API key appears in a Git commit, anyone with access to the repository can use it — and if the repository is public (or becomes public through an accidental push), the key is exposed to the world. Environment variables are the standard mechanism for injecting secrets into a Python program without storing them in files.
Reading Environment Variables

import os

# Read an environment variable — returns None if not set
epex_key = os.environ.get('CELTICWIND_EPEX_API_KEY')

# Read with a default value for optional settings
log_level = os.environ.get('CELTICWIND_LOG_LEVEL', 'INFO')

# Read a required variable — raise an error if not set
epex_key = os.environ.get('CELTICWIND_EPEX_API_KEY')
if epex_key is None:
    raise RuntimeError(
        'Missing required environment variable: CELTICWIND_EPEX_API_KEY. '
        'Set it in your .env file or export it in your shell.'
    )

The os.environ dictionary contains every environment variable that was set when the Python process started. The get() method returns the value if the variable exists and None (or a default) if it does not. For required secrets like the EPEX API key, the pipeline checks that the variable is set and raises a clear error if it is missing — far better than a confusing KeyError or a silent failure deeper in the code. At CelticWind, the naming convention prefixes every variable with CELTICWIND_ to avoid collisions with other applications' variables.
The .env File for Local Development

# .env — local development secrets (NEVER committed to Git)
CELTICWIND_EPEX_API_KEY=sk_test_abc123def456
CELTICWIND_SLACK_WEBHOOK=https://hooks.slack.com/services/T00/B00/xxx
CELTICWIND_LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG

During local development, setting environment variables in the shell every time you open a terminal is tedious. The .env file provides a shortcut: it stores the variables in a file that the python-dotenv library loads automatically at startup. The .env file is listed in .gitignore so it is never committed to Git. Each developer creates their own .env file with their own API key. On the production server, the variables are set by the deployment system (Kubernetes secrets, AWS Parameter Store, or Azure Key Vault) — the .env file is only for local development.

# Loading .env automatically at pipeline startup
from dotenv import load_dotenv
import os

# Load variables from .env file into os.environ
load_dotenv()  # Looks for .env in the current directory

# Now os.environ contains the variables from .env
epex_key = os.environ.get('CELTICWIND_EPEX_API_KEY')
# epex_key is now 'sk_test_abc123def456'

blocks.title: The .gitignore check is non-negotiable
blocks.content: Before committing any code, verify that .env appears in your .gitignore file. A single accidental commit of a .env file exposes every secret in the Git history — even if you delete the file in a later commit, the old commit still contains it. At CelticWind, the project template (lesson 10) includes .env in .gitignore from the start, and the pre-commit hook (lesson 9) rejects any commit that includes a file named .env.

Every CelticWind pipeline file can be used in two ways: as a module (imported by another file for its functions) or as a script (run directly from the command line). The pipeline.py file is imported by the test suite (to test its functions) and also run directly by the scheduler (to execute the pipeline). Python needs to distinguish between these two uses — when the file is imported, it should define functions without executing the pipeline. When the file is run directly, it should execute the pipeline. The if __name__ == '__main__' pattern makes this distinction.
How It Works

# src/celticwind/pipeline.py
import logging
from pathlib import Path
from celticwind.readers import read_csv_meters
from celticwind.validators import validate_readings

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def run_silver_pipeline(config: dict) -> None:
    """Transform Bronze meter readings to Silver."""
    bronze_path = Path(config['paths']['bronze_dir'])
    readings = read_csv_meters(bronze_path)
    valid, rejected = validate_readings(readings)
    logger.info('Valid: %d, Rejected: %d', len(valid), len(rejected))
    # ... rest of pipeline


# This block runs ONLY when the file is executed directly
# It does NOT run when the file is imported
if __name__ == '__main__':
    from celticwind.config import load_config
    config = load_config(Path('config/config.yml'))
    run_silver_pipeline(config)

When Python runs a file directly (python src/celticwind/pipeline.py), it sets the special variable __name__ to the string '__main__'. When Python imports a file (from celticwind.pipeline import run_silver_pipeline), it sets __name__ to the module's dotted name ('celticwind.pipeline'). The if __name__ == '__main__' check uses this distinction: the code inside the if block runs only when the file is executed directly, not when it is imported. This means the test suite can import run_silver_pipeline without triggering the actual pipeline execution.
Why This Pattern Matters for Testing

Without the __name__ guard, importing pipeline.py would execute the pipeline — reading files, calling APIs, and writing output. This makes the file untestable: you cannot import a function to test it without also triggering the full pipeline. With the guard, the import brings in the function definitions without side effects. The test file can import run_silver_pipeline, call it with test data, and verify the output — without touching any real files or APIs. At CelticWind, every file that can be run as a script uses the __name__ guard. Files that are pure libraries (readers.py, validators.py) do not need it because they are never run directly.

blocks.title: The guard is the boundary between definition and execution
blocks.content: Above the if __name__ == '__main__' line: function definitions, class definitions, constant declarations — things that describe what the code can do. Below the line: the actual execution — loading config, calling the pipeline function, handling top-level errors. This boundary makes the file simultaneously a reusable library (when imported) and an executable program (when run directly).

When two developers write Python, they make different style choices: one uses single quotes, the other uses double quotes. One puts spaces around operators, the other does not. One writes long lines, the other wraps at 88 characters. These differences are harmless individually but create noisy Git diffs: a commit that changes one line of logic also changes 20 lines of formatting, making it hard to see what actually changed. Linting and formatting tools eliminate this noise by enforcing consistent style automatically — every developer's code looks the same, so diffs show only logic changes.
ruff — The Linter

# Run ruff to check for common errors and style violations
# ruff check src/ tests/

# Example output:
# src/celticwind/readers.py:14:5: F841 local variable 'header' is assigned but never used
# src/celticwind/validators.py:28:1: E302 expected 2 blank lines before function definition
# src/celticwind/pipeline.py:7:1: F401 'os' imported but unused

# Fix auto-fixable issues:
# ruff check --fix src/ tests/

ruff is a fast Python linter that checks for common errors: unused variables, unused imports, missing blank lines, overly complex expressions, and potential bugs. Each finding has a code (F841, E302, F401) that links to a detailed explanation. The --fix flag automatically corrects issues that have a safe fix (removing unused imports, adding blank lines). At CelticWind, ruff runs on every commit via a pre-commit hook — catching style issues before they reach the repository.
black — The Formatter

# Run black to format all Python files
# black src/ tests/

# Example: before black
def filter_readings( readings,min_kw,region ):
    return [r for r in readings if r['daily_kw']>=min_kw and r['region']==region]

# After black
def filter_readings(readings, min_kw, region):
    return [
        r
        for r in readings
        if r["daily_kw"] >= min_kw and r["region"] == region
    ]

black is an opinionated code formatter — it reformats your code to a single consistent style with no configuration options. Spaces around operators, consistent quoting, line wrapping at 88 characters, and trailing commas in multi-line structures are all applied automatically. The 'no configuration' philosophy eliminates style debates: you do not argue about formatting because black decides for everyone. At CelticWind, black runs alongside ruff in the pre-commit hook, so every committed file has consistent formatting regardless of which developer wrote it.
Pre-Commit Hooks

A pre-commit hook is a script that runs automatically before every Git commit. If the hook fails (ruff finds an error, black reformats a file), the commit is blocked until the issues are fixed. This means that every commit in the repository passes the linting and formatting checks — no exceptions, no 'I will fix it later.' At CelticWind, the pre-commit configuration runs ruff check, black --check (verify without changing), and a check that no .env file is being committed. The hook adds approximately 2 seconds to each commit — a negligible cost for the consistency it provides.

blocks.title: Automation beats discipline
blocks.content: You could ask every developer to run ruff and black before committing. Some would forget. Some would skip it when they are in a hurry. The pre-commit hook removes the choice: the tools run automatically on every commit, and the commit fails if the code does not pass. Automation is more reliable than good intentions — especially at 03:00 when someone is hotfixing a pipeline and does not want to think about formatting.

This module introduced nine tools: virtual environments for isolation, pip and requirements.txt for dependency management, project layout for organization, modules and imports for code structure, type hints for documentation and safety, YAML for configuration, environment variables for secrets, the __name__ guard for dual-use files, and linting/formatting for code quality. Each tool solves a specific problem. Together, they form the scaffolding that makes pipeline code professional — portable, reproducible, maintainable, and safe. This final lesson assembles all nine into the CelticWind project template that every code example in the remaining modules follows.
The Complete Template

# Setting up the CelticWind project from scratch
# Every command in this block is run once when starting a new project

# 1. Create the project directory
# mkdir celticwind-pipeline && cd celticwind-pipeline

# 2. Create the virtual environment
# python3 -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate

# 3. Create the directory structure
# mkdir -p src/celticwind tests config data/sample
# touch src/celticwind/__init__.py tests/__init__.py tests/conftest.py

# 4. Create the .gitignore
# echo '.venv/\n__pycache__/\n.env\n*.pyc\n.pytest_cache/' > .gitignore

# 5. Install dependencies
# pip install pyarrow requests pydantic PyYAML pytest pytest-cov ruff black python-dotenv
# pip freeze > requirements.txt

# 6. Create the .env file (local secrets)
# echo 'CELTICWIND_EPEX_API_KEY=sk_test_abc123' > .env

# 7. Initialize Git
# git init && git add . && git commit -m 'Initial project setup'

The template is a sequence of seven steps that produce a fully configured project in under 5 minutes. After step 7, the project has an isolated environment (.venv), pinned dependencies (requirements.txt), a clean directory structure (src/celticwind/ and tests/), secrets management (.env with .gitignore protection), and version control (Git initialized with the first commit). Every code example from Module 3 onward lives inside this structure — the reader module goes into src/celticwind/readers.py, the tests go into tests/test_readers.py, and the configuration goes into config/config.yml.
The pyproject.toml

# pyproject.toml — project metadata and tool configuration
[project]
name = "celticwind-pipeline"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "CelticWind Energy Silver pipeline"
requires-python = ">=3.12"

[tool.ruff]
line-length = 88
target-version = "py312"

[tool.black]
line-length = 88
target-version = ["py312"]

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
testpaths = ["tests"]
pythonpath = ["src"]

The pyproject.toml file is the project's identity card: it declares the project name, version, Python version requirement, and the configuration for the development tools (ruff, black, pytest). The [tool.pytest.ini_options] section tells pytest where to find the tests (tests/) and where to find the source code (src/) — which is necessary for the src layout from lesson 3. At CelticWind, pyproject.toml is the single file that every tool reads for its configuration — no separate setup.cfg, no tox.ini, no pytest.ini.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: The foundation phase is complete. You understand the job (Module 1) and the tools (Module 2). Module 3 begins the core mechanics phase with data structures for tabular data — lists of dictionaries, named tuples, and dataclasses. These are the Python representations of CelticWind's meter readings: the data structures that every function in the pipeline receives, transforms, and returns. The project template from this lesson is the scaffolding; Module 3 starts filling it with pipeline logic.

chapters.title: Data Structures for Tabular Data
chapters.description: Master the Python data structures that represent tabular data in pipeline code: lists of dictionaries, named tuples, dataclasses, and the patterns for iterating, filtering, transforming, and aggregating collections of records.

blocks.content: A data pipeline processes rows of data — meter readings, turbine measurements, weather observations. Each row has named fields: a meter reading has a meter_id, a daily_kw, a date, a region, and a customer_type. In Python, the most natural way to represent a row with named fields is a dictionary: the keys are the field names and the values are the field values. This is the representation you will use for most of this course, and it is the representation that Python's built-in csv.DictReader (Module 5) produces when it reads a CSV file.
A CelticWind Meter Reading

# A single meter reading as a dictionary
reading = {
    'meter_id': 'IE-DUB-00142',
    'daily_kw': 28.5,
    'date': '2025-01-15',
    'region': 'DUBLIN',
    'customer_type': 'RESIDENTIAL',
}

# Access a field by its key
print(reading['daily_kw'])       # 28.5
print(reading['region'])         # DUBLIN

# Safely access a field that might not exist
substation = reading.get('substation_id', 'UNKNOWN')
print(substation)                # UNKNOWN (key not present, default returned)

The dictionary stores each field with a descriptive name. When you write reading['daily_kw'], you know exactly which field you are accessing — unlike a list, where reading[1] requires you to remember that index 1 is the daily kilowatts. The get() method provides safe access: if the key does not exist (substation_id is not in this dictionary), it returns the default value instead of raising a KeyError. This safe access pattern is essential for pipeline code, where source data sometimes has missing or unexpected fields.
Why Dictionaries for Data

Dictionaries are the default choice for representing data records in Python for three reasons. First, field access is by name — reading['region'] is self-documenting, while reading[3] is not. Second, dictionaries handle missing fields gracefully with get(). Third, Python's JSON and CSV libraries produce dictionaries natively — json.load() returns dictionaries, and csv.DictReader produces dictionaries. Starting with dictionaries means you can read data from any source and start processing it immediately, without converting to a different structure.

blocks.title: Dictionaries are flexible but unstructured
blocks.content: A dictionary does not enforce its shape: you can add, remove, or rename keys at any time. This flexibility is useful when you do not know the schema in advance (Bronze layer data), but dangerous when you need guarantees (Silver layer data). Later in this module (lessons 6-8), you will learn named tuples and dataclasses — structures that enforce their shape and provide the guarantees that Silver-layer pipeline code needs.

A single meter reading is one dictionary. A day's worth of meter readings — 400,000 of them — is a list of 400,000 dictionaries. The list-of-dictionaries structure is the most common way to represent tabular data in Python pipeline code: the list represents the table (ordered rows), and each dictionary represents a row (named fields). This structure is what csv.DictReader produces, what json.load() returns for a JSON array of objects, and what most Python data processing code expects as input.
A Small Table

# Five meter readings as a list of dictionaries
readings = [
    {'meter_id': 'IE-DUB-00142', 'daily_kw': 28.5, 'date': '2025-01-15', 'region': 'DUBLIN', 'customer_type': 'RESIDENTIAL'},
    {'meter_id': 'IE-DUB-00143', 'daily_kw': 45.2, 'date': '2025-01-15', 'region': 'DUBLIN', 'customer_type': 'COMMERCIAL'},
    {'meter_id': 'IE-CRK-00201', 'daily_kw': 12.8, 'date': '2025-01-15', 'region': 'CORK', 'customer_type': 'RESIDENTIAL'},
    {'meter_id': 'IE-GAL-00089', 'daily_kw': 0.0,  'date': '2025-01-15', 'region': 'GALWAY', 'customer_type': 'RESIDENTIAL'},
    {'meter_id': 'IE-BEL-00310', 'daily_kw': 67.3, 'date': '2025-01-15', 'region': 'BELFAST', 'customer_type': 'INDUSTRIAL'},
]

# Basic operations
print(len(readings))             # 5 (number of rows)
print(readings[0]['meter_id'])   # IE-DUB-00142 (first row's meter ID)
print(readings[-1]['daily_kw'])  # 67.3 (last row's daily kW)

The list provides ordered access: readings[0] is the first row, readings[-1] is the last row, and len(readings) is the row count. Each element is a dictionary, so readings[0]['meter_id'] accesses the first row's meter_id field. This two-level access pattern — index into the list to get a row, key into the dictionary to get a field — is the fundamental operation for tabular data in Python. Every transformation in this module uses this pattern.
Iterating Over Rows

# Print each reading's meter ID and consumption
for reading in readings:
    print(f"{reading['meter_id']}: {reading['daily_kw']} kW")

# Output:
# IE-DUB-00142: 28.5 kW
# IE-DUB-00143: 45.2 kW
# IE-CRK-00201: 12.8 kW
# IE-GAL-00089: 0.0 kW
# IE-BEL-00310: 67.3 kW

The for loop iterates over the list, assigning each dictionary to the variable reading. Inside the loop, reading['meter_id'] and reading['daily_kw'] access the current row's fields. The f-string formats the output as a readable line. This pattern — for row in table, then access fields within row — is the most common iteration pattern in data pipeline code. Every filtering, transformation, and aggregation operation in the next three lessons is built on top of this loop.

blocks.title: Lists of dictionaries are memory-hungry
blocks.content: Each dictionary stores a copy of every key name ('meter_id', 'daily_kw', etc.) alongside the values. For 400,000 rows with 5 fields, that is 400,000 × 5 = 2 million copies of the key strings. For small datasets (under 1 million rows), this overhead is negligible. For larger datasets, named tuples and dataclasses (lessons 6-7) reduce the overhead significantly. For truly large datasets (millions of rows), frameworks like PySpark and pandas handle the memory management for you.

Filtering is the operation of selecting specific rows from a collection based on a condition. CelticWind's Bronze meter readings contain all 400,000 records for the day. The Silver pipeline needs to filter out rows with missing or invalid values: readings where daily_kw is negative (physically impossible — a meter cannot consume negative energy), readings where the region is not one of the four valid regions, and readings where the meter_id is missing. Filtering produces a new, smaller collection that contains only the rows that pass the condition.
The For-Loop-with-If Pattern

The for loop examines each row, the if statement checks the condition, and append() adds matching rows to a new list. The original readings list is unchanged — filtering produces a new collection without modifying the input. This immutability pattern (new output, unchanged input) is the same principle that PySpark's filter() follows — and the same reason the medallion architecture keeps Bronze data untouched while producing Silver data in a separate location.
The List Comprehension Pattern

# The same filter as a list comprehension — more concise, same result
dublin_residential = [
    reading for reading in readings
    if reading['region'] == 'DUBLIN'
    and reading['customer_type'] == 'RESIDENTIAL'
]

# Filter to remove invalid readings (daily_kw must be non-negative)
valid_readings = [
    reading for reading in readings
    if reading['daily_kw'] >= 0
]

# Filter to keep only readings from valid regions
valid_region_readings = [
    reading for reading in readings
    if reading['region'] in valid_regions
]

print(len(valid_readings))         # 5 (all readings have daily_kw >= 0)
print(len(valid_region_readings))  # 5 (all regions are valid)

The list comprehension [reading for reading in readings if condition] is a compact way to write the for-loop-with-if pattern. It reads almost like English: 'give me each reading from readings if the condition is true.' For simple conditions (one or two checks), the comprehension is more readable than the loop. For complex conditions (multiple checks with different logic branches), the loop is clearer because each check can be on its own line with comments. At CelticWind, Aoife's style guide uses comprehensions for single-condition filters and loops for multi-condition filters.
Combining Multiple Filters

The function checks three conditions in priority order: missing meter_id first, then negative daily_kw, then invalid region. Each rejected reading gets a reject_reason field added (using the {**reading, 'reject_reason': '...'} dictionary merge pattern) so that the quarantine file records why the reading was rejected. The function returns both the valid and rejected lists — the pipeline writes valid readings to Silver and rejected readings to the quarantine directory. This two-output pattern is the foundation of the validation framework in Module 8.

blocks.title: Filters never modify the original data
blocks.content: Every filter in this lesson creates a new list. The original readings list is never changed. This is not just good style — it is a safety pattern. If a filter modifies the input, the next filter sees the modified data instead of the original. Debugging a pipeline where each step modifies the shared data structure is extremely difficult because the bug in step 3 may have been caused by step 1. Keeping each step's input unchanged makes every step independently debuggable.

Filtering selects which rows to keep. Transforming changes the rows themselves: adding new fields, converting types, reformatting values, and computing derived values. CelticWind's Bronze-to-Silver transformation converts daily_kw from a string (as it arrives in the CSV) to a float, normalizes region codes to uppercase, adds a daily_mwh field (daily_kw divided by 1,000), and adds a loaded_at timestamp recording when the pipeline processed the record. Each transformation produces a new dictionary — the original row is unchanged.
Transforming a Single Row

from datetime import datetime, timezone

def transform_reading(raw: dict) -> dict:
    """Transform a Bronze meter reading to Silver format."""
    daily_kw = float(raw['daily_kw'])  # string to float
    return {
        'meter_id': raw['meter_id'],
        'daily_kw': daily_kw,
        'daily_mwh': daily_kw / 1000,  # derived field
        'date': raw['date'],
        'region': raw['region'].upper(),  # normalize to uppercase
        'customer_type': raw['customer_type'].upper(),
        'loaded_at': datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
    }

# Transform one reading
raw_reading = {
    'meter_id': 'IE-DUB-00142',
    'daily_kw': '28.5',  # Note: string, as it arrives from CSV
    'date': '2025-01-15',
    'region': 'Dublin',  # Note: mixed case
    'customer_type': 'Residential',
}

silver_reading = transform_reading(raw_reading)
print(silver_reading['daily_kw'])     # 28.5 (float, not string)
print(silver_reading['daily_mwh'])    # 0.0285
print(silver_reading['region'])       # DUBLIN (uppercase)

The transform_reading function takes a raw (Bronze) dictionary and returns a new (Silver) dictionary. The raw dictionary is not modified — the function constructs a new dictionary with the transformed values. The float() call converts the string '28.5' to the number 28.5. The .upper() call normalizes 'Dublin' to 'DUBLIN'. The daily_mwh field is computed from daily_kw. The loaded_at field records the current UTC timestamp. Each of these transformations is a common pattern: type conversion, case normalization, derived field computation, and audit timestamp addition.
Transforming a Collection

# Transform all readings using a list comprehension
silver_readings = [transform_reading(r) for r in readings]

# Or equivalently, with a for loop
silver_readings = []
for raw in readings:
    silver_readings.append(transform_reading(raw))

print(len(silver_readings))                    # 5
print(silver_readings[0]['daily_mwh'])          # 0.0285
print(silver_readings[0]['region'])             # DUBLIN

The comprehension [transform_reading(r) for r in readings] applies the transformation function to every row, producing a new list of Silver-formatted dictionaries. This is the map pattern: apply a function to each element of a collection, producing a new collection of the same length with transformed elements. The map pattern is the most common transformation pattern in data engineering — and it is directly analogous to PySpark's withColumn() (which applies a transformation to every row of a DataFrame) and SQL's SELECT with expressions (which computes new columns from existing ones).
Handling Transformation Errors

The safe_transform_reading wrapper catches transformation errors — a daily_kw value of 'N/A' that cannot be converted to float, a missing region key, or an unexpected type. Instead of crashing the pipeline, it logs the error and returns None. The subsequent filter removes the None values, producing a clean list of successfully transformed readings and a count of failures.
